Algorithms for stochastic non-convex optimization are a foundational element of modern neural network training.
Choice of optimization algorithm (and the associated hyperparameters) is critical for achieving good performance of the underlying model, as well as training stability.

There are few formal rules for choosing optimizers and hyperparameters, with researchers and practitioners typically defaulting to a small number of optimizers and associated hyperparameters with which they are familiar.
Algorithm performance varies substantially across different real world tasks.

Learned optimizers have been proposed, which are typically composed of blackbox function approximators (such as neural networks) or hand-designed functions containing hyperparameters that are learned.
Learned optimizers typically contain hyperparameters that are learned.
The meta-training of these optimizers, in which they are trained to optimize some metric of performance for the underlying model such as validation loss, has strong similarities to training.
Learned optimizers and validation loss have a relationship in terms of performance evaluation for the underlying model.
Learned optimizers are related to neural network models, as they are used to train the models and can impact their stability.
Learned optimizers can have reduced performance and stability when applied in circumstances unlike those in which they are meta-trained.
Instability and inconsistency may arise in the meta-training of learned optimizers, impacting their performance and dependence on random seed.
Tools from dynamical systems theory are used to characterize the stability of parameter dynamics induced by learned optimizers.
Changes in the architecture and training of learned optimizers are proposed to improve stability and inductive bias, including incorporating a nominal optimizer and using weight decay.
The weight decay on the optimizer’s parameters is mentioned as a method to stabilize the updates generated by the learned optimizer.
Preconditioning the updates generated by the learned optimizer before applying them to the parameters is stated as a technique to improve stability.
The resulting stabilized through ample regularization (STAR) learned optimizer is highlighted as being more stable and faster to meta-train.
The STAR learned optimizer is compared to baseline learned optimizers, indicating its stability and performance advantages.
STAR generalizes well to new tasks, such as a transformer task with more parameters and training steps than the MLP it was trained to optimize.

Intervention magnitude is increased by incorporating a nominal term, causing eigenvalues to have a more negative real part and to decay toward the real line.
Increasing weight decay pulls the entries in P toward 0, turning off the learned optimizer and thus pulls the eigenvalues of A toward 1.
Pre-conditioning reduces the impact of the problem's Hessian H, leading to eigenvalues that depend more on properties of the optimizer, and less on properties of the problem being optimized.

The focus is on guaranteeing stability of the update dynamics, which requires ensuring a certain condition (A) < 1. This condition can be related to eigenvalues of matrix P, assuming they are real and diagonalizable. Symmetric matrices are common for preconditioners, allowing for a clear order of eigenvalues, leading to stability considerations.

The eigenvalues of matrix P play a crucial role in analyzing stability. When P has real eigenvalues and is diagonalizable, stability considerations become clearer. Symmetric matrices are commonly used for preconditioners.

The ordering of eigenvalues of matrix A plays a significant role in determining stability. The minimum and maximum eigenvalues are important for stability analysis, especially when considering the stabilizing effect of the nominal optimization term.

The introduction of a nominal optimization term affects stability considerations. It can provide additional stability margin by preventing divergence in certain directions. However, there is a tradeoff as it may lead to a loss of stability margin in other cases.
